lines: Pause. [long pause] Wherever you are right now — at your desk, in your car, standing in a kitchen, sitting on a bench, walking between meetings — pause. [long pause] The day is halfway through, and you haven't stopped to notice it yet. [long pause] So stop. [long pause] Just for a few minutes.

Take a breath. [long pause] A slow one. [long pause] In through your nose... [long pause] [long pause] two... [long pause] [long pause] three... [long pause] [long pause] four... [long pause] [long pause] five.

And out through your mouth... [long pause] [long pause] two... [long pause] [long pause] three... [long pause] [long pause] four... [long pause] [long pause] five... [long pause] [long pause] six... [long pause] [long pause] seven.

Again. [long pause] In... [long pause] [long pause] letting your shoulders drop as you fill your lungs. [long pause] Letting the morning's tension start to drain... [long pause] [long pause] four... [long pause] [long pause] five.

And out... [long pause] [long pause] letting the first half of the day leave your body with the breath... [long pause] [long pause] five... [long pause] [long pause] six... [long pause] [long pause] seven.
One more. [long pause] In... [long pause] [long pause] noticing your chest expand, your ribs widen, your belly soften.
And out... [long pause] [long pause] noticing everything settle. [long pause] The body saying: okay. [long pause] We're pausing. [long pause] Finally.

Let your breathing find its own rhythm now. [long pause] You don't need to control it. [long pause] Just notice that it's happening — that your body has been breathing for you all morning. [long pause] Thousands of breaths since you woke up, every one of them a gift you didn't ask for and couldn't earn.

Feel where you are. [long pause] The surface holding you up — a chair, the floor, the seat of your car, the ground beneath your feet. [long pause] Feel the temperature of the air on your skin. [long pause] The sounds of this particular place, this particular hour. [long pause] You're in the middle of a day, in the middle of a life, and you've stopped to look around. [long pause] That matters.
"This is the day that the Lord has made; let us rejoice and be glad in it." Psalm one-eighteen, verse twenty-four.

Not tomorrow. [long pause] Not the day you're planning for. [long pause] Not the perfect day you keep waiting for — the one where everything is in order, where the inbox is empty, where you finally feel on top of things. [long pause] That day isn't coming. [long pause] This one is here. [long pause] With all its interruptions and small annoyances and unremarkable moments and hidden graces — this is the day.

We're going to spend these next few minutes doing something inspired by the Ignatian Examen — a practice that Ignatius of Loyola taught nearly five hundred years ago. [long pause] He considered it the most important prayer of the day, more important even than the Mass. [long pause] And he placed it at midday and evening — not morning, but the middle and end of the day. [long pause] Because this isn't a prayer about planning or hoping or setting intentions. [long pause] It's a prayer about paying attention to what has already happened. [long pause] Looking back over the hours you've already lived and asking: where was God in this? [long pause] Where was the grace I missed because I was too busy to notice?

Ignatius used two words. [long pause] Consolation — the moments where your heart expanded, where something felt right, where you felt alive or grateful or connected, even briefly. [long pause] And desolation — the moments where your heart contracted, where you felt drained or anxious or hollow or far from God. [long pause] Both are important. [long pause] Both reveal something. [long pause] We'll look for both.

Let your mind travel back to the beginning of this day. [long pause] The very first moments. [long pause] The alarm, or the light through the window, or the sounds that woke you. [long pause] Don't rush. [long pause] Move through the morning slowly, like paging through a photo album. [long pause] Not analyzing. [long pause] Just... [long pause] [long pause] remembering.

Start with the very first good thing. [long pause] It might have been tiny — so small that you didn't even register it at the time. [long pause] The warmth of the bed before you got out of it. [long pause] The first sip of something hot. [long pause] The feeling of water on your face. [long pause] A moment of quiet before the noise started. [long pause] Light coming through a window at a particular angle.
Let it come to you. [long pause] Don't force it. [long pause] Just scan gently, like running your hand over fabric, feeling for the texture that catches.

When you find it, stay there. [long pause] Don't move on yet. [long pause] Feel it again — not just the memory, but the sensation. [long pause] The warmth. [long pause] The taste. [long pause] The quality of the light. [long pause] Let your body re-experience it.

Where does it register? [long pause] Where in your body do you feel the echo of that good thing? [long pause] Warmth in your chest? [long pause] A softening in your shoulders? [long pause] A settling in your stomach? [long pause] Something around the eyes?

This was a consolation. [long pause] A moment of grace. [long pause] Small, ordinary, easy to miss — and God was in it. [long pause] Not dramatically. [long pause] Not with thunder and angels. [long pause] Just... [long pause] [long pause] quietly present in the warmth of a cup, the kindness of the light, the mercy of a body that woke up one more time.

Now think of a person. [long pause] Someone you encountered this morning or early afternoon — in person, on a call, over a message, even in passing. [long pause] Someone who made the day a little better. [long pause] A little warmer. [long pause] A little less alone.

It might be someone who listened. [long pause] Someone who made you laugh. [long pause] A coworker who handled something so you didn't have to. [long pause] A stranger who was patient. [long pause] A child who said something absurd and true. [long pause] Someone who simply showed up and did their job with grace, making your life easier without knowing it.
See their face. [long pause] Remember the specific moment — what was said, or the tone of voice, or the expression, or the simple fact of their presence.

What would this day have been without them? [long pause] Not dramatically worse, maybe. [long pause] But emptier. [long pause] Thinner. [long pause] Less human. [long pause] That person is a gift — an unearned, undeserved, ordinary gift. [long pause] They didn't owe you that kindness, that patience, that laugh. [long pause] But they gave it. [long pause] And you received it, probably without even thinking about it.
Silently: thank you. [long pause] For them. [long pause] That they exist. [long pause] That your paths crossed today. [long pause] That the world contains people like this.

Now your body. [long pause] Your body has been working since you woke up — since before you woke up — and you probably haven't thanked it once. [long pause] We treat our bodies like machines, noticing them only when they break. [long pause] But your body has been carrying you faithfully all morning.

It digested your food. [long pause] It carried you from room to room. [long pause] It processed a thousand sensory signals every second without asking your permission. [long pause] Your heart has been beating — tens of thousands of times since you woke up. [long pause] You didn't have to ask. [long pause] You didn't have to remember. [long pause] It just... [long pause] [long pause] kept going. [long pause] The most loyal thing in your life, and the one you're least grateful for.

God, thank you for this day. [long pause] The real one — not the one I planned, not the one I wished for, not the one I'm still hoping will magically materialize after lunch. [long pause] The one I actually got. [long pause] This one. [long pause] With all of it.

Thank you for the good in it. [long pause] The small moments I almost missed because I was rushing. [long pause] The person who made it less lonely. [long pause] The body that carried me here, still beating, still breathing, still somehow working after everything I ask of it.

And thank you — though I say this with some hesitation, with an honest lump in my throat — for the hard parts too. [long pause] Not because I enjoyed them. [long pause] Not because they were good. [long pause] But because even in the difficulty, you were there. [long pause] Even when I didn't notice. [long pause] Even when I couldn't feel it. [long pause] You were there.

Teach me to pay attention. [long pause] Not just in this meditation, not just in the quiet moments, but in the noise and the rush and the ordinary middle of a day that doesn't feel special but is. [long pause] Help me catch the next gift before it passes. [long pause] Help me notice the next person before I forget to be grateful. [long pause] Help me see the burning bush in the boring meeting, the grace in the grocery line, the presence of God in the place I least expect it.

And when this day ends, let me look back and see — not a day wasted, not a day merely survived, but a day lived. [long pause] A day full of ordinary, sacred things I almost overlooked but didn't. [long pause] Because I paused. [long pause] Because I looked. [long pause] Because you taught me to see.
Thank you. [long pause] For all of it. [long pause] For this breath, right now, which I did not earn and do not deserve and cannot explain. [long pause] Thank you.
Take one more breath. [long pause] Slow. [long pause] Full. [long pause] All the way in. [long pause] All the way out.

Feel your body. [long pause] Feel the ground beneath you. [long pause] The air on your skin. [long pause] The sounds of the world going about its business around you, oblivious to the fact that you just stopped in the middle of it to say thank you.

The second half of the day is ahead of you. [long pause] You don't have to carry the morning's difficulties into it — you've already looked at them, held them, and given thanks in the midst of them. [long pause] That's enough. [long pause] You can set them down now.

As you move into the afternoon, carry one intention with you — just one. [long pause] Notice one more thing today. [long pause] One moment of beauty. [long pause] One act of kindness. [long pause] One small grace you would normally walk right past. [long pause] Not because optimism is a virtue or because positivity fixes everything. [long pause] But because attention is a form of prayer. [long pause] And you just prayed. [long pause] And it was honest. [long pause] And it was real.
This is the day the Lord has made. [long pause] It matters more than you think.
